# /buyer-needs-assessment

Builds a buyer consultation guide that forces a real distinction between dealbreakers and nice-to-haves, and surfaces any gap between the buyer's wish list and their actual budget before showings start.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

A new buyer client is about to have their first consultation and the agent wants a structured guide to run the meeting - most valuable when it prevents the common failure mode of touring a dozen homes before discovering the buyer's real budget doesn't match their must-have list.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Buyer's stated budget and whether they've been pre-approved (and by whom, and for how much)
- Their stated wish list (location, size, style, features) as given so far
- Timeline (are they buying because of a lease ending, a job relocation, a life event - this affects urgency and flexibility)
- Whether this is their first home purchase (changes how much process education the consultation needs to include)

## Workflow

1. Start with financing reality, not house features - confirm pre-approval status and amount before discussing wants; a wish list built on an unconfirmed budget wastes everyone's time touring homes the buyer can't actually get financed for.
2. Run every wish-list item through a real dealbreaker test: "if you found the perfect home in every other way but it didn't have this, would you walk away?" Only true walk-away items go in "must-have" - everything else, however strongly stated, goes in "preferred."
3. Explicitly probe for the tension between location, size, condition, and price - most buyers want all four maximized and need to hear directly, with real local market context, which one or two they'll likely need to flex on.
4. Surface timeline-driven urgency or flexibility honestly (e.g. a lease ending in 6 weeks changes the whole search strategy vs. someone who's "just starting to look") so showings are scheduled at the right pace.
5. Ask about deal-breaker property types or conditions separately from style preferences (e.g. busy road, HOA, fixer-upper willingness, flood zone tolerance) since these often get missed in a features-only conversation and cause wasted showings.
6. Close the consultation by reflecting the finalized must-have list back to the buyer in writing, so both sides have the same reference point going into the search - this also gives the agent a document to filter listings against instead of relying on memory.

## Output format

```
# Buyer Needs Assessment - [Buyer Name(s)]
Date: [date]

## Financing
Pre-approved: Y/N | Amount: $[X] | Lender: [name]
Target price range: $[low] - $[high]

## Timeline & motivation
Reason for buying: [___] | Target move date: [___] | Flexibility: [low/med/high]

## Must-haves (true dealbreakers - passed the walk-away test)
- [item]
- [item]

## Preferred (nice-to-have, flexible)
- [item]
- [item]

## Known flex points (buyer acknowledged willingness to compromise)
- [e.g. "will consider smaller lot for better school district"]

## Hard no's (property types/conditions to exclude from search)
- [e.g. busy road, no HOA tolerance, must have garage]

## Agent notes / market reality check
[Any gap between wish list and budget/inventory to address directly with buyer]
```

## Do
- Confirm financing and pre-approval before discussing house features
- Apply a real walk-away test to separate must-haves from preferences
- Name the specific tradeoff (location/size/condition/price) the buyer will likely face

## Don't
- Don't let every wish-list item get labeled "must-have" - that defeats the purpose of the assessment
- Don't skip the hard-no property conditions section; it prevents wasted showings
- Don't avoid telling the buyer directly if their must-have list and budget don't match current inventory

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Must-have list only contains items that passed the explicit walk-away test
- [ ] Financing/pre-approval status is captured before feature preferences
- [ ] Any budget-vs-wish-list gap is called out directly, not glossed over
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /buyer-needs-assessment or describe the task from the skill description.

# /closing-checklist-client-update

Builds a stage-by-stage closing checklist tied to this transaction's actual dates and contingencies, then turns it into a plain-language client email that separates "done," "in progress," and "needs your action."

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

A transaction is under contract and moving toward closing, and the agent wants to keep the client (buyer or seller) informed at a key milestone - inspection results in, appraisal ordered, loan conditions cleared, or just a routine "where do things stand" update - without the client having to guess what's next or feeling anxious from silence.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Whether this is for a buyer or seller client
- Contract dates: inspection deadline, appraisal, loan commitment date, closing date
- Current status of each contingency (cleared, pending, an issue arose)
- Anything specific the client needs to do right now (schedule a walkthrough, sign a document, wire funds, get insurance binder to lender)

## Safety

- This is a status update and organizational tool, not legal or lending advice - advise the client to direct any questions about contract deadlines, contingency rights, or loan terms to the transaction attorney (where customary) or their lender directly.
- Propose before acting: share the draft update with the user first; wait for explicit user confirmation before it is sent to the client.
- Never take an irreversible action (waiving a contingency, confirming a closing date change) without the user explicitly saying to proceed.

## Workflow

1. Build the full closing checklist first, anchored to the actual contract dates for this transaction (inspection deadline, appraisal, loan commitment, final walkthrough, closing) rather than a generic template timeline - every date should be this deal's real date.
2. Mark each checklist item's real current status: done, in progress (with expected completion), or blocked/at-risk - vague "things are moving along" updates are what erode client trust, specific status doesn't.
3. Separate what the agent/other parties are handling from what specifically requires the client's action right now - a client update that buries their one required action inside a wall of process narration is the update that causes missed deadlines.
4. If anything is at risk (financing issue, inspection dispute, appraisal came in low), flag it plainly and pair it with what happens next, rather than either hiding it or presenting it in a way that reads as alarming without context.
5. Translate the checklist into a warm, plain-language client email - no industry jargon (no "CD," "clear to close," "contingency removal" without a one-line explanation) since clients, especially first-timers, often don't know these terms.
6. End the email with the next expected touchpoint so the client knows when they'll hear from the agent again, reducing anxious "just checking in" messages in between.

## Output format

```
## Internal closing checklist - [Property Address]
| Milestone | Contract date | Status | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Inspection | ... | Done/In progress/At risk | ... |
| Inspection resolution | ... | ... | ... |
| Appraisal | ... | ... | ... |
| Loan commitment | ... | ... | ... |
| Final walkthrough | ... | ... | ... |
| Closing | ... | ... | ... |

---
## Client email

Subject: [subject line - specific to this update, e.g. "Update on 123 Main St - Appraisal Complete"]

[Warm opening]
[Plain-language status: what's done, what's in progress]
[Clear callout: what the client needs to do right now, if anything, with a deadline]
[Anything at-risk, explained plainly with next steps]
[When they'll hear from you next]
```

## Do
- Anchor every checklist date to this transaction's real contract dates
- Separate "needs your action" clearly from general process narration
- Explain any jargon in plain language for the client

## Don't
- Don't bury the client's required action inside a long narrative paragraph
- Don't hide an at-risk item to avoid an uncomfortable conversation - flag it with next steps
- Don't send the update without confirming current status is actually accurate/current

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Disclaimer present directing contract/lending questions to attorney/lender
- [ ] Approval gate present before the email is sent to the client
- [ ] Client's specific required action (if any) is clearly separated and impossible to miss
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /closing-checklist-client-update or describe the task from the skill description.

# /market-comparison-summary

Turns a list of comparable sales and active listings into a client-facing narrative that explains, in plain language, why the recommended price range makes sense - not just a spreadsheet of numbers with no story.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

An agent has pulled comparable sales (from MLS or provided by the user) for a listing presentation or pricing conversation and needs to turn raw comp data into something a seller or buyer can actually follow and trust, especially when the comps don't perfectly agree with each other.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- The subject property's key details (beds/baths/sqft/lot/condition/upgrades)
- The comp list: address, sold or active price, sold date (if sold), sqft, condition/upgrade notes, days on market
- Whether this is for a seller (pricing strategy conversation) or a buyer (offer strategy conversation) - framing differs
- Any market condition context (rising/falling/flat, current inventory levels, interest rate environment) that affects how much weight to put on older comps

## Safety

- This is a market-pricing opinion based on available comparable data, not a formal appraisal - advise the client that a licensed appraiser's valuation is the authoritative number for lending, legal, or estate purposes.
- Propose before acting: share the draft summary and recommended range with the user before it's presented to the client, so the agent can sanity-check figures against their own MLS access.
- Never take an irreversible action (submitting a listing price, making representations that bind the client) without the user's explicit confirmation.

## Workflow

1. Adjust each comp conceptually for the subject property's differences (bigger/smaller, updated/dated, better/worse lot or location) and state the adjustment logic in plain words - e.g. "this comp sold $20k higher but has a renovated kitchen ours doesn't, so treat it as a ceiling, not a target."
2. Weight recent, nearby, and similar-condition comps more heavily than older or dissimilar ones, and say so explicitly - a 6-month-old sale in a fast-moving market should visibly carry less weight than one from 6 weeks ago.
3. Include active listings as competition context, not just closed comps - a seller needs to know what they're up against right now, not only what sold months ago.
4. Translate days-on-market patterns into a plain-language read on demand (e.g. "comps under $X sold in under 2 weeks; above $X, over 45 days" ) so the client understands the pricing "cliff," not just an average.
5. Land on a recommended range (not a single number) with the reasoning spelled out - a range that's defensible against the comps, framed as "priced to sell in X weeks" vs. "priced to test the market," so the client understands the tradeoff they're choosing.
6. Flag any comp that's an outlier and explain why it was excluded or down-weighted (distressed sale, off-market deal, unusually motivated buyer/seller) so the client doesn't fixate on a number that doesn't reflect normal market conditions.

## Output format

```
# Market Comparison Summary - [Subject Property Address]
Prepared for: [Seller/Buyer name]  |  Date: [date]

## Subject property snapshot
[beds/baths/sqft/lot/condition/key upgrades]

## Comparable sales
| Address | Sold price | Sold date | Sqft | Condition notes | DOM | Adjustment logic |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|

## Active competition (for context)
| Address | List price | Sqft | DOM so far | How it compares |
|---|---|---|---|---|

## What the comps say
[Plain-language narrative: which comps matter most and why, the days-on-market "cliff" if one exists, any outliers excluded]

## Recommended price range
$[low] - $[high]
Reasoning: [tradeoff between speed and price, tied to comp evidence]

Note: this is a market-based pricing opinion, not a formal appraisal.
```

## Do
- Explain the adjustment logic for each comp in plain words, not just raw numbers
- Weight recent, similar, nearby comps more heavily and say so
- Present a range with an explicit speed-vs-price tradeoff, not a single number with no context

## Don't
- Don't present a CMA as an appraisal or guarantee of sale price
- Don't average all comps blindly without accounting for condition or recency differences
- Don't hide or ignore outlier comps - name them and explain why they're excluded

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Disclaimer present distinguishing this from a formal appraisal
- [ ] Every comp has stated adjustment logic, not just a raw number
- [ ] Recommended range includes reasoning tied to specific comp evidence
```

6. Flag any comp that's an outlier and explain why it was excluded or down-weighted (distressed sale, off-market deal, unusually motivated buyer/seller) so the client doesn't fixate on a number that doesn't reflect normal market conditions.

## Output format

```

Prepared for: [Seller/Buyer name]  |  Date: [date]

## Subject property snapshot
[beds/baths/sqft/lot/condition/key upgrades]

## Comparable sales
| Address | Sold price | Sold date | Sqft | Condition notes | DOM | Adjustment logic |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|

## Active competition (for context)
| Address | List price | Sqft | DOM so far | How it compares |
|---|---|---|---|---|

## What the comps say
[Plain-language narrative: which comps matter most and why, the days-on-market "cliff" if one exists, any outliers excluded]

## Recommended price range
$[low] - $[high]
Reasoning: [tradeoff between speed and price, tied to comp evidence]

Note: this is a market-based pricing opinion, not a formal appraisal.
```

## Do
- Explain the adjustment logic for each comp in plain words, not just raw numbers
- Weight recent, similar, nearby comps more heavily and say so
- Present a range with an explicit speed-vs-price tradeoff, not a single number with no context

## Don't
- Don't present a CMA as an appraisal or guarantee of sale price
- Don't average all comps blindly without accounting for condition or recency differences
- Don't hide or ignore outlier comps - name them and explain why they're excluded

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Disclaimer present distinguishing this from a formal appraisal
- [ ] Every comp has stated adjustment logic, not just a raw number
- [ ] Recommended range includes reasoning tied to specific comp evidence

# /mls-listing-description

Writes an MLS-ready listing description that leads with the property's single strongest differentiator, hits key searchable terms buyers filter on, and stays within typical MLS character limits.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

A listing just went live or is about to, and the agent needs the actual MLS remarks field written - not a marketing blog post, but the specific compact copy that has to work inside a character limit and also feed portal search filters (Zillow, Realtor.com) correctly.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Property basics: beds/baths/sqft/lot size, year built, key systems (roof, HVAC age if recently replaced)
- The single most compelling feature - the thing that will make a buyer stop scrolling (view, renovation, location, school district, unique layout)
- Any recent updates/renovations with approximate dates, since "updated kitchen" without a date reads as unverifiable
- Neighborhood or lifestyle context that isn't already implied by the address
- The MLS's character limit if known (varies by board, commonly 800-1,000 characters) and any fair housing restrictions the local board enforces

## Workflow

1. Identify the single strongest differentiator and lead with it in the first sentence - MLS remarks get truncated in portal previews, so whatever doesn't survive the first ~100 characters is often never read.
2. Write in the terse, high-density MLS register (sentence fragments are normal and expected here) rather than flowing marketing prose - this is a different register than a social post or a brochure.
3. Weave in searchable, filterable terms buyers and portals key on (e.g. "open floor plan," "primary suite on main," "finished basement," specific school district name if locally relevant) since these terms drive portal search matches, not just human readability.
4. State recent updates with approximate timing ("roof 2023," "kitchen renovated 2022") rather than vague adjectives like "updated" or "newer" - specific and recent claims are more persuasive and more defensible if a buyer later checks.
5. Run a fair housing compliance check: remove any language referencing protected classes or steering implications (no "perfect for families," "ideal for a young couple," "walking distance to church," "safe neighborhood") - replace with property-fact language instead (e.g. "near [named park]" instead of "great for kids").
6. Trim to the character limit, protecting the lead sentence and the strongest 2-3 features first if something has to be cut.

## Output format

```
# MLS Description - [Address]
Character count: [X] / [limit]

[Full listing description, ready to paste into MLS remarks field]

---
## Key searchable terms included
[list, so agent can verify against MLS/portal filter fields]

## Fair housing check
[Confirm no protected-class or steering language remains]
```

## Do
- Lead with the single strongest differentiator in the first sentence
- Use specific dates for renovations/updates, not vague adjectives
- Include portal-searchable terms buyers actually filter on

## Don't
- Don't use any language that references or implies a preference for a protected class (family status, religion, national origin, etc.) - this is a fair housing compliance risk, not just a style choice
- Don't exceed the MLS character limit and force manual truncation later
- Don't rely on generic adjectives ("beautiful," "must-see") without a specific fact backing them up

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Fair housing language check explicitly performed and confirmed clean
- [ ] Character count fits within stated or typical MLS limit
- [ ] Lead sentence contains the property's actual strongest differentiator, not generic filler
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /mls-listing-description or describe the task from the skill description.

# /neighborhood-guide-draft

Writes a neighborhood guide organized around the specific questions relocating or first-time buyers actually ask (commute, schools, walkability, safety perception, community feel) with a clear flag on anything the agent should verify rather than state as fact.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

An agent wants area content for a listing page, a buyer packet, or ongoing lead-gen/SEO content, and needs something that reads like genuine local knowledge rather than a generic template that could describe any suburb in the country.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- The specific neighborhood/area name and its actual boundaries (not just a city name)
- The agent's real local knowledge: commute patterns, specific parks/amenities, restaurant or shopping strips, notable annual events, housing stock character (era, style, typical lot sizes)
- Target audience for this guide (families with school-age kids, young professionals, retirees, investors) since the framing changes significantly
- Whether this is for a specific listing (tie it to that home) or general area content (broader, SEO-oriented)

## Safety

- Fair housing rules restrict language that steers buyers based on protected characteristics - never frame content around who a neighborhood is "good for" in terms of family status, religion, race, national origin, or similar; describe amenities and facts, and let the buyer draw their own conclusions.
- Flag any factual claim (school ratings, crime statistics, specific commute times) that the agent should verify against a current, named source before publishing - don't present unverified guesses as settled fact.
- Propose before acting: share the draft for the user's review; wait for explicit confirmation before it's published anywhere public-facing.

## Workflow

1. Organize the guide around the real questions buyers ask when relocating to or considering an unfamiliar area - commute/transit, school district structure (name the district, not a subjective rating), everyday amenities (grocery, parks, gyms), and neighborhood character/housing stock - rather than a generic "vibrant community" opener.
2. Write specific, checkable details wherever possible (named parks, named restaurant strips, named annual events, actual distances/drive times) instead of vague superlatives like "great neighborhood feel" that could describe anywhere.
3. Run a fair housing language check: remove any phrasing that frames the area around who it's suited for by protected characteristic (no "perfect for young families," "ideal for retirees," "safe for kids") - replace with neutral factual framing (parks, schools by name, walkability) and let readers self-select.
4. Flag any statistic (school ratings, crime data, specific commute times) with a note to verify against a current, named source (school district site, city data, a mapping tool) before publishing - don't let the draft present an unverified number as fact.
5. If this is tied to a specific listing, connect 2-3 neighborhood details directly to that property (e.g. "a 5-minute walk to the same park visible from the backyard") so the guide feels attached to this home, not bolted on generically.
6. Close with a genuine local-expertise touch - something only someone who actually knows the area would mention (a specific seasonal event, a locally-loved but not-famous spot) since this is what differentiates real local content from AI-generated-sounding filler.

## Output format

```
# [Neighborhood Name] Guide

## Overview
[2-3 sentences: character, housing stock, era, general feel - specific, not generic]

## Getting around
[Commute patterns, transit options, drive times to key hubs]

## Schools
[District name(s) - flagged for user to verify current ratings/boundaries]

## Everyday life
[Named grocery/shopping, parks, gyms, notable restaurants]

## Community & events
[Named annual events, local traditions]

## [If tied to a listing] Why this fits this home
[2-3 specific connections between the neighborhood and the property]

## Facts to verify before publishing
- [ ] [any school rating, crime stat, or commute time cited]
```

## Do
- Use specific, named, checkable details instead of generic superlatives
- Flag any stat that needs source verification before publishing
- Tie the guide to a specific listing when applicable

## Don't
- Don't frame the area around who it's "ideal for" by family status, religion, or other protected characteristics - fair housing risk
- Don't state school ratings, crime stats, or commute times as fact without a verification flag
- Don't write generic "vibrant, sought-after neighborhood" copy that could apply to any area

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Fair housing language check explicitly performed and confirmed clean
- [ ] All statistics/ratings are flagged for verification, not stated as unchecked fact
- [ ] Content contains specific, named, checkable details rather than generic filler
```

# /offer-negotiation-talking-points

Builds a negotiation talking-points sheet for an active offer, tailored to which side the agent represents and to the actual leverage points in this specific deal - financing strength, timeline, contingencies, competing offers - not a generic negotiation script.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

An offer just came in (or is about to go out) and the agent needs to prepare their client and their own approach for the negotiation conversation - most useful when there's a real gap to close (price, contingencies, timeline, repairs) and the agent wants a clear-eyed view of actual leverage rather than a generic "always counter" reflex.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Which side the agent represents (buyer or seller) in this transaction
- The offer terms: price, financing type, contingencies, proposed closing date, any concessions requested
- The client's real priorities and flexibility (is price the only thing that matters, or is timeline/certainty more important than top dollar)
- Market context: is this a multiple-offer situation, how long has the property been listed, how strong is the buyer's financing

## Safety

- This is negotiation strategy support, not legal advice on contract terms - advise the client to have their real estate attorney (where customary) or the agent's broker review any contingency, disclosure, or contract-specific language before signing.
- Propose before acting: share the talking points with the user first; wait for explicit user/client confirmation before any counter-offer or acceptance is communicated to the other side.
- Never take an irreversible action (submitting a counter, accepting terms) without the user explicitly saying to proceed.

## Workflow

1. Map the real leverage in this specific deal first: for sellers, that's competing offers, low days-on-market, or strong comps; for buyers, that's weak competition, financing strength, or seller motivation (relocation, already-purchased-elsewhere, price reductions already taken) - leverage should come from real facts about this deal, not a generic "always hold firm" posture.
2. Identify which terms are actually most important to the client (price vs. closing timeline vs. contingency removal vs. repair credits) so the negotiation optimizes for what the client cares about, not just the headline number.
3. Prepare a specific counter strategy with reasoning attached (e.g. "counter $X above ask, citing comps at $Y and $Z, since two of the three closest comps support this price") rather than an arbitrary round-number counter.
4. Prepare responses to the likely reactions from the other side (they hold firm, they counter back partway, they walk) so the client isn't blindsided by any of the three common outcomes.
5. If contingencies or repair requests are part of the negotiation, separate "must resolve" from "willing to concede" for the client in advance, the same way a buyer needs-assessment separates dealbreakers from preferences.
6. Prepare a walk-away framing for the client - the point at which continuing to negotiate costs more (in time, relationship, or opportunity cost) than the marginal gain is worth - so the client enters the conversation with a real ceiling/floor already decided, not decided emotionally mid-negotiation.

## Output format

```
# Negotiation Talking Points - [Property Address]
Representing: [Buyer/Seller] - [Client Name]

## Deal snapshot
Offer/ask: $[X] | Financing: [type] | Contingencies: [list] | Proposed close: [date]

## Real leverage in this deal
[Specific facts: competing offers, DOM, comps, financing strength, motivation signals]

## Client's real priorities (ranked)
1. [priority]
2. [priority]

## Recommended counter strategy
Proposed terms: [specifics]
Reasoning: [tied to comps/leverage above]

## Contingencies/repairs: must-resolve vs. willing-to-concede
Must-resolve: [list]
Willing to concede: [list]

## If they hold firm / counter partway / walk
- Hold firm: [response]
- Counter partway: [response]
- Walk: [response]

## Client's walk-away point
[Explicit ceiling/floor, decided in advance]
```

## Do
- Ground leverage claims in real facts about this specific deal
- Rank the client's true priorities before proposing counter terms
- Prepare for all three likely reactions from the other side, not just the hoped-for one

## Don't
- Don't recommend a generic "always counter" or "never counter" posture regardless of the deal's actual leverage
- Don't let the negotiation optimize for price alone if the client actually cares more about timeline or certainty
- Don't finalize or communicate any counter without the client's explicit sign-off first

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Disclaimer present noting contract-specific terms should go through attorney/broker review
- [ ] Approval gate present before any counter is communicated
- [ ] Leverage points are specific to this deal's real facts, not generic negotiation advice
```

An offer just came in (or is about to go out) and the agent needs to prepare their client and their own approach for the negotiation conversation - most useful when there's a real gap to close (price, contingencies, timeline, repairs) and the agent wants a clear-eyed view of actual leverage rather than a generic "always counter" reflex.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Which side the agent represents (buyer or seller) in this transaction
- The offer terms: price, financing type, contingencies, proposed closing date, any concessions requested
- The client's real priorities and flexibility (is price the only thing that matters, or is timeline/certainty more important than top dollar)
- Market context: is this a multiple-offer situation, how long has the property been listed, how strong is the buyer's financing

## Safety

- This is negotiation strategy support, not legal advice on contract terms - advise the client to have their real estate attorney (where customary) or the agent's broker review any contingency, disclosure, or contract-specific language before signing.
- Propose before acting: share the talking points with the user first; wait for explicit user/client confirmation before any counter-offer or acceptance is communicated to the other side.
- Never take an irreversible action (submitting a counter, accepting terms) without the user explicitly saying to proceed.

## Workflow

1. Map the real leverage in this specific deal first: for sellers, that's competing offers, low days-on-market, or strong comps; for buyers, that's weak competition, financing strength, or seller motivation (relocation, already-purchased-elsewhere, price reductions already taken) - leverage should come from real facts about this deal, not a generic "always hold firm" posture.
2. Identify which terms are actually most important to the client (price vs. closing timeline vs. contingency removal vs. repair credits) so the negotiation optimizes for what the client cares about, not just the headline number.
3. Prepare a specific counter strategy with reasoning attached (e.g. "counter $X above ask, citing comps at $Y and $Z, since two of the three closest comps support this price") rather than an arbitrary round-number counter.
4. Prepare responses to the likely reactions from the other side (they hold firm, they counter back partway, they walk) so the client isn't blindsided by any of the three common outcomes.
5. If contingencies or repair requests are part of the negotiation, separate "must resolve" from "willing to concede" for the client in advance, the same way a buyer needs-assessment separates dealbreakers from preferences.
6. Prepare a walk-away framing for the client - the point at which continuing to negotiate costs more (in time, relationship, or opportunity cost) than the marginal gain is worth - so the client enters the conversation with a real ceiling/floor already decided, not decided emotionally mid-negotiation.

## Output format

```
# Negotiation Talking Points - [Property Address]
Representing: [Buyer/Seller] - [Client Name]

## Deal snapshot
Offer/ask: $[X] | Financing: [type] | Contingencies: [list] | Proposed close: [date]

## Real leverage in this deal
[Specific facts: competing offers, DOM, comps, financing strength, motivation signals]

## Client's real priorities (ranked)
1. [priority]
2. [priority]

## Recommended counter strategy
Proposed terms: [specifics]
Reasoning: [tied to comps/leverage above]

## Contingencies/repairs: must-resolve vs. willing-to-concede
Must-resolve: [list]
Willing to concede: [list]

## If they hold firm / counter partway / walk
- Hold firm: [response]
- Counter partway: [response]
- Walk: [response]

## Client's walk-away point
[Explicit ceiling/floor, decided in advance]
```

## Do
- Ground leverage claims in real facts about this specific deal
- Rank the client's true priorities before proposing counter terms
- Prepare for all three likely reactions from the other side, not just the hoped-for one

## Don't
- Don't recommend a generic "always counter" or "never counter" posture regardless of the deal's actual leverage
- Don't let the negotiation optimize for price alone if the client actually cares more about timeline or certainty
- Don't finalize or communicate any counter without the client's explicit sign-off first

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Disclaimer present noting contract-specific terms should go through attorney/broker review
- [ ] Approval gate present before any counter is communicated
- [ ] Leverage points are specific to this deal's real facts, not generic negotiation advice

# /open-house-followup-email

Writes three distinct open-house follow-up emails segmented by visitor type - genuinely interested buyer, casual/early-stage looker, and neighbor or non-buyer visitor - matched to what each type actually wants next.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

An agent just wrapped an open house with a sign-in sheet of names and wants to send follow-ups the same day, while the property is still fresh in visitors' minds - most effective when segmented rather than sending one identical blast to everyone who walked through.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- The property address and 2-3 standout features to reference
- Sign-in sheet notes: who asked detailed questions (financing, timeline, comparable homes), who was vague/browsing, who mentioned they live nearby
- Whether the agent is the listing agent (can speak with full authority on the property) or a buyer's agent host
- Any time-sensitive info to include (other offers pending, an upcoming price change, a second open house date)

## Workflow

1. Segment the sign-in list into three buckets based on observed behavior, not just guesswork: hot leads (asked about financing, timeline, or said "we need to move fast"), casual lookers (early in their search, vague answers, "just looking"), and neighbors/non-buyers (said they live nearby or aren't planning to buy).
2. For hot leads, write a direct, specific follow-up referencing what they actually asked about at the house, and propose a concrete next step (private showing, sending comps, a call to discuss financing) - don't waste their momentum on generic "thanks for stopping by."
3. For casual lookers, write a lower-pressure email that keeps the door open (offer to send similar listings, ask about their timeline and criteria) without pushing for an immediate commitment they're not ready for.
4. For neighbors/non-buyers, write a short, warm note that plants a seed for future referral or listing business ("if you ever think about what your own home could sell for, happy to run the numbers") rather than pretending they're a buyer lead.
5. Send (recommend sending) all three same-day - open house follow-ups lose most of their effectiveness after 24-48 hours while the visit is still top of mind.
6. Add each contact to the appropriate nurture cadence going forward (immediate follow-up call for hot leads, a periodic check-in for casual lookers, an occasional market-update touch for neighbors) so this isn't a one-off email with no next step.

## Output format

```
## Segment 1: Hot lead - [Name]
Subject: [subject line]
[Email body referencing their specific questions + concrete next step/CTA]

## Segment 2: Casual looker - [Name]
Subject: [subject line]
[Email body, lower pressure, keeps door open]

## Segment 3: Neighbor/non-buyer - [Name]
Subject: [subject line]
[Short warm note, plants referral/future-listing seed]

## Suggested next touch per segment
- Hot leads: [e.g. call within 24 hours]
- Casual lookers: [e.g. add to monthly listing alert]
- Neighbors: [e.g. quarterly market update touch]
```

## Do
- Segment based on actual observed behavior at the open house, not a single generic template
- Reference specific questions or comments each visitor made
- Send same-day while the visit is fresh

## Don't
- Don't send the same high-pressure "let's schedule a showing" email to someone who was clearly just browsing
- Don't ignore neighbors as dead leads - they're a real source of future listings and referrals
- Don't wait more than a day or two to send; open house follow-up value decays fast

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] All three segments are meaningfully different, not the same email with a name swapped
- [ ] Each email references something specific from that visitor's actual visit
- [ ] A concrete next step or nurture cadence is proposed for every segment
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /open-house-followup-email or describe the task from the skill description.

# /past-client-referral-checkin

Writes a personalized past-client check-in tied to a real, specific moment (closing anniversary, a life event the agent knows about, a market shift relevant to their home) that reconnects genuinely, with any referral or business ask kept secondary and low-pressure.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

An agent wants to stay top-of-mind with a past client - most naturally around a closing anniversary, a birthday, a life event (new baby, upcoming move for a family member), or a market change that specifically affects their home's value - without sending a transparent "please refer me" ask that damages the relationship.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Which past client and what triggered this check-in (anniversary date, life event, market news relevant to their specific home/area)
- Any personal detail worth referencing (kids' names/ages, a home project they mentioned wanting to do, their pet) that makes the message feel human, not automated
- Whether there's a genuinely useful piece of information to share (an updated home value estimate, a relevant market shift, a service provider recommendation) rather than just a hollow "thinking of you"
- Whether a referral or review ask is appropriate this time, or whether this touch should be pure relationship maintenance with no ask at all

## Workflow

1. Anchor the message to one real, specific trigger - a closing anniversary date, a genuine life update the agent remembers, or a market fact specifically relevant to their property - rather than a generic "just checking in" that reads as an automated drip email.
2. Lead with something useful or personal to the client, not with the agent's business - e.g. "your home's estimated value has moved noticeably since we closed, thought you'd want to know" gives real value before any ask appears.
3. If a referral or review ask is included at all, make it small, specific, and easy to decline - a single sentence near the end, not the focus of the message, and never framed as owed or expected.
4. Avoid making every touch a referral ask - if this is a frequent cadence (e.g. quarterly), rotate between pure value touches (market update, local recommendation) and touches that include a soft ask, so the relationship doesn't read as transactional every time.
5. Keep the tone as a genuine relationship message a friend would send, not corporate marketing copy - short, warm, specific, no forced enthusiasm or exclamation-point-heavy language.
6. Close with an easy, low-effort way to respond (a quick reply, not a scheduling link or a form) since past clients are far more likely to respond to something that feels like a real conversation starter.

## Output format

```
## Check-in message - [Client Name]
Trigger: [anniversary/life event/market news]
Channel: [email/text]

Subject (if email): [subject line]

[Message body: personal/specific opener, genuine value or update,
optional small easy-to-decline ask, warm closing]

## Notes for the agent
Ask included this time: [Y/N - and what]
Suggested next touch: [timing and type, e.g. "pure value touch in 3 months"]
```

## Do
- Anchor every message to one real, specific, verifiable trigger
- Lead with something useful or personal before any ask
- Keep any referral/review ask small, specific, and easy to decline

## Don't
- Don't send a generic "just checking in, hope all is well!" with no real content
- Don't make every single touch a referral ask - vary pure-value touches with occasional asks
- Don't use corporate marketing tone in what should read as a personal note

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Message is anchored to one specific, real trigger, not generic filler
- [ ] Any ask included is small, secondary, and easy to decline
- [ ] Tone reads as a personal note, not a marketing template
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /past-client-referral-checkin or describe the task from the skill description.

# /seller-consultation-prep

Builds a seller consultation prep packet: agenda, value proposition tailored to this seller's actual situation, and specific rebuttals to the objections most likely to come up (price expectations, commission, timing) rather than generic sales talking points.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

An agent has a listing appointment scheduled (especially a competitive one where the seller is interviewing multiple agents) and needs to walk in with a tailored agenda and objection-handling plan instead of a generic pitch deck.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- The seller's situation and motivation (why selling, timeline, whether they've already gotten a price idea from Zillow/another agent, any known emotional attachment or urgency)
- The property's real market position (from comps if available) - especially whether the seller's price expectation looks realistic or inflated
- Whether the seller is interviewing other agents (changes how differentiation needs to be framed)
- The agent's actual differentiators for this specific seller (marketing plan, local track record, past sales in this neighborhood) rather than generic claims

## Workflow

1. Open the agenda with the seller's stated goals and timeline first, not the agent's credentials - sellers remember agents who clearly understood their situation, not agents who led with a resume.
2. Identify the specific price-expectation gap if one exists (e.g. seller has a number in mind from a Zestimate or a neighbor's sale) and prepare a grounded, comp-based explanation for the real number, rather than a vague "the market will decide."
3. Prepare a commission conversation that addresses value, not just defends the number - tie the fee to specific, concrete parts of the marketing and negotiation plan for this listing, so it isn't an abstract percentage debate.
4. Anticipate the "why you vs. the other agent(s)" question directly if this is a competitive listing interview, and prepare a specific, honest differentiator (not generic "I work harder") tied to something checkable - recent local sales, a specific marketing tactic, responsiveness, negotiation track record.
5. Prepare a timing/urgency conversation grounded in actual current market conditions (inventory levels, days-on-market trends, rate environment) rather than manufactured pressure - sellers see through artificial urgency and it damages trust.
6. Build a short list of questions the agent should ask the seller during the meeting (not just answers to deliver) - a consultation that's all agent-talking is a worse close than one that surfaces the seller's real concerns first.

## Output format

```
# Seller Consultation Prep - [Address] - [Seller Name(s)]
Appointment: [date/time]

## Seller's situation (confirm/adjust)
Motivation: [why selling] | Timeline: [target] | Known price expectation: [if any]

## Agenda
1. [Seller's goals/timeline discussion]
2. [Market position / pricing conversation]
3. [Marketing plan walkthrough]
4. [Commission/value conversation]
5. [Next steps / listing agreement]

## Anticipated objections & responses
| Objection | Likely reason it comes up | Grounded response |
|---|---|---|
| Price expectation too high | [reason] | [comp-based response] |
| Commission pushback | [reason] | [value-tied response] |
| "Why you over [other agent]" | [reason] | [specific differentiator] |
| Timing/urgency skepticism | [reason] | [market-data response] |

## Questions to ask the seller during the meeting
- [question]
- [question]
```

## Do
- Ground every price and urgency claim in actual comp/market data, not generic pressure
- Prepare specific, checkable differentiators, not vague "I work harder" claims
- Include real questions to ask the seller, not just a monologue script

## Don't
- Don't manufacture urgency the current market doesn't actually support
- Don't defend the commission rate in the abstract without tying it to specific marketing/negotiation value
- Don't walk in assuming the seller's price expectation is correct or incorrect without checking real comps first

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Every objection response is grounded in this seller's actual data, not generic script
- [ ] Agenda opens with the seller's situation, not the agent's credentials
- [ ] At least 2-3 genuine discovery questions for the agent to ask the seller are included
```

# /seller-consultation-prep

Builds a seller consultation prep packet: agenda, value proposition tailored to this seller's actual situation, and specific rebuttals to the objections most likely to come up (price expectations, commission, timing) rather than generic sales talking points.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

An agent has a listing appointment scheduled (especially a competitive one where the seller is interviewing multiple agents) and needs to walk in with a tailored agenda and objection-handling plan instead of a generic pitch deck.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- The seller's situation and motivation (why selling, timeline, whether they've already gotten a price idea from Zillow/another agent, any known emotional attachment or urgency)
- The property's real market position (from comps if available) - especially whether the seller's price expectation looks realistic or inflated
- Whether the seller is interviewing other agents (changes how differentiation needs to be framed)
- The agent's actual differentiators for this specific seller (marketing plan, local track record, past sales in this neighborhood) rather than generic claims

## Workflow

1. Open the agenda with the seller's stated goals and timeline first, not the agent's credentials - sellers remember agents who clearly understood their situation, not agents who led with a resume.
2. Identify the specific price-expectation gap if one exists (e.g. seller has a number in mind from a Zestimate or a neighbor's sale) and prepare a grounded, comp-based explanation for the real number, rather than a vague "the market will decide."
3. Prepare a commission conversation that addresses value, not just defends the number - tie the fee to specific, concrete parts of the marketing and negotiation plan for this listing, so it isn't an abstract percentage debate.
4. Anticipate the "why you vs. the other agent(s)" question directly if this is a competitive listing interview, and prepare a specific, honest differentiator (not generic "I work harder") tied to something checkable - recent local sales, a specific marketing tactic, responsiveness, negotiation track record.
5. Prepare a timing/urgency conversation grounded in actual current market conditions (inventory levels, days-on-market trends, rate environment) rather than manufactured pressure - sellers see through artificial urgency and it damages trust.
6. Build a short list of questions the agent should ask the seller during the meeting (not just answers to deliver) - a consultation that's all agent-talking is a worse close than one that surfaces the seller's real concerns first.

## Output format

```
# Seller Consultation Prep - [Address] - [Seller Name(s)]
Appointment: [date/time]

## Seller's situation (confirm/adjust)
Motivation: [why selling] | Timeline: [target] | Known price expectation: [if any]

## Agenda
1. [Seller's goals/timeline discussion]
2. [Market position / pricing conversation]
3. [Marketing plan walkthrough]
4. [Commission/value conversation]
5. [Next steps / listing agreement]

## Anticipated objections & responses
| Objection | Likely reason it comes up | Grounded response |
|---|---|---|
| Price expectation too high | [reason] | [comp-based response] |
| Commission pushback | [reason] | [value-tied response] |
| "Why you over [other agent]" | [reason] | [specific differentiator] |
| Timing/urgency skepticism | [reason] | [market-data response] |

## Questions to ask the seller during the meeting
- [question]
- [question]
```

## Do
- Ground every price and urgency claim in actual comp/market data, not generic pressure
- Prepare specific, checkable differentiators, not vague "I work harder" claims
- Include real questions to ask the seller, not just a monologue script

## Don't
- Don't manufacture urgency the current market doesn't actually support
- Don't defend the commission rate in the abstract without tying it to specific marketing/negotiation value
- Don't walk in assuming the seller's price expectation is correct or incorrect without checking real comps first

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] Every objection response is grounded in this seller's actual data, not generic script
- [ ] Agenda opens with the seller's situation, not the agent's credentials
- [ ] At least 2-3 genuine discovery questions for the agent to ask the seller are included

# /social-post-for-new-listing

Writes distinct new-listing announcement posts for Instagram, Facebook, and LinkedIn, each matched to that platform's format and audience behavior instead of one caption copy-pasted everywhere.

## Works with

Use this as a portable `SKILL.md` in Grok, Claude, Cursor, Codex, VS Code agent skills, or any AI workspace that supports reusable markdown instructions. If a tool does not support slash commands, paste the task and ask the AI to follow this skill.

## When to use

A new listing just hit the market (or is about to) and the agent wants to announce it across their social channels same-day, with copy tuned to each platform instead of a single generic caption posted three places.

## Inputs to gather

Check context first; ask at most one question if a missing detail changes the answer.
- Property address, price, beds/baths/sqft, and the single strongest visual/lifestyle hook
- Whether photos/video are ready (affects whether to write for a carousel, reel, or single image post)
- Open house date/time if one is scheduled
- Whether the agent wants to include price publicly (some agents prefer "DM for details" for competitive-offer strategy)
- Any local hashtag/geo-tag conventions the agent already uses

## Workflow

1. Write the Instagram version around a visual-first hook line (short, punchy, stops the scroll) with a caption that tells a quick lifestyle story about the home, not a spec sheet - specs go in a follow-up comment or the last lines, not the opening.
2. Write the Facebook version longer and more descriptive than Instagram - Facebook audiences (often more local, more likely to be past clients or neighbors) respond better to fuller detail and a direct, conversational tone, plus a clear call-to-action for open house attendance or DMs.
3. Write the LinkedIn version reframed as professional/market commentary, not a consumer sales pitch - LinkedIn audiences are often other professionals or past clients in a business context, so frame it around market insight or the agent's business (e.g. "excited to list this one - here's what's driving demand in this area right now") rather than pure listing-ad copy.
4. Add 3-5 relevant local hashtags/geo-tags for Instagram (neighborhood name, city, #realtor-style tags) calibrated to what actually gets local search traction, not generic high-volume tags with no local relevance.
5. Include a clear, single call-to-action per post (DM for a private showing, link in bio, open house time) - don't stack three different CTAs in one caption.
6. If an open house is scheduled, make sure the date/time is prominent and repeated near the top of each caption, not buried at the end where it gets cut off in a "see more" truncation.

## Output format

```
## Instagram
[Hook line]
[Caption body - lifestyle-first, short]
Hashtags: [list]
CTA: [single clear action]

## Facebook
[Caption body - fuller detail, conversational]
CTA: [single clear action]

## LinkedIn
[Caption body - market/professional framing]
CTA: [single clear action]
```

## Do
- Write each platform version distinctly, matched to that audience's behavior
- Keep the open house date/time prominent if one exists
- Use one clear call-to-action per post

## Don't
- Don't paste the identical caption across all three platforms
- Don't lead Instagram with specs instead of a visual/lifestyle hook
- Don't stack multiple competing CTAs in a single post

## Quality check (before responding)
- [ ] Output is copy-paste-ready
- [ ] Works outside Grok - no unexplained tool-specific assumption left in the text
- [ ] All three platform versions are meaningfully different in tone and structure
- [ ] Each post has exactly one clear call-to-action
- [ ] Open house date/time (if applicable) is prominent, not buried
```

After writing the file, confirm it exists. The user can then run /social-post-for-new-listing or describe the task from the skill description.
